Impact Evaluation in Practice Chapter Map

Chapter-level reading map for Impact Evaluation in Practice.

Source

This map organises the generated chapter notes for Impact Evaluation in Practice (Gertler et al. 2016).

Reading Route

  • Start with policy fit: Chapters 1-2 clarify why and how to prepare for impact evaluation.
  • Build the causal design: Chapters 3-10 cover counterfactual logic, five main methods, implementation threats, and multifaceted programs.
  • Implement responsibly: Chapters 11-14 connect method choice to management, ethics, transparency, and dissemination.
  • Secure data quality: Chapters 15-16 cover sampling, power, data sources, fieldwork, and storage.

Chapters

Chapter Source Pages Main Design Question Link
1. Why Evaluate? 33-59 Impact evaluation is justified when a policy decision needs credible causal evidence about whether a program works, for whom, and under what implementation choices. chapter-01-why-evaluate.qmd
2. Preparing for an Evaluation 61-73 A credible evaluation starts by turning program logic into a results chain, evaluation questions, and measurable indicators before any method is chosen. chapter-02-preparing-for-an-evaluation.qmd
3. Causal Inference and Counterfactuals 77-93 The core problem of impact evaluation is constructing a valid estimate of the counterfactual: what would have happened to treated units without treatment. chapter-03-causal-inference-and-counterfactuals.qmd
4. Randomized Assignment 97-116 Randomized assignment creates treatment and comparison groups by chance, making average outcome differences a strong estimate of program impact when implementation preserves the design. chapter-04-randomized-assignment.qmd
5. Instrumental Variables 119-140 Instrumental variables recover causal impact when assignment or encouragement changes participation, especially when not everyone complies with the intended treatment status. chapter-05-instrumental-variables.qmd
6. Regression Discontinuity Design 143-157 Regression discontinuity estimates impacts by comparing units just above and below a program eligibility cutoff, where units should be similar except for eligibility. chapter-06-regression-discontinuity-design.qmd
7. Difference-in-Differences 159-171 Difference-in-differences estimates impact by comparing outcome changes over time between treatment and comparison groups, relying on the assumption that their trends would otherwise have moved together. chapter-07-difference-in-differences.qmd
8. Matching 173-188 Matching builds a comparison group from untreated units with similar observed characteristics, but it cannot eliminate bias from unobserved differences without stronger assumptions or complementary designs. chapter-08-matching.qmd
9. Addressing Methodological Challenges 191-202 Design problems such as imperfect compliance, spillovers, and attrition must be anticipated and analyzed because they can change what the estimated impact means. chapter-09-addressing-methodological-challenges.qmd
10. Evaluating Multifaceted Programs 205-213 Multifaceted programs can be evaluated by designing treatment arms that compare levels, components, or combinations of components, not only treatment versus no treatment. chapter-10-evaluating-multifaceted-programs.qmd
11. Choosing an Impact Evaluation Method 217-229 Method choice should start from program rules, timing, and data, then select the strongest feasible design with the weakest necessary assumptions. chapter-11-choosing-an-impact-evaluation-method.qmd
12. Managing an Impact Evaluation 231-259 Impact evaluation management requires a documented plan that coordinates policy questions, design, sampling, data, analysis, ethics, dissemination, budget, timeline, and team roles. chapter-12-managing-an-impact-evaluation.qmd
13. The Ethics and Science of Impact Evaluation 261-275 Ethical impact evaluation protects participants and fair access while open science practices protect the credibility, reliability, and usefulness of results. chapter-13-the-ethics-and-science-of-impact-evaluation.qmd
14. Disseminating Results and Achieving Policy Impact 277-287 Policy impact depends on planning dissemination early, asking decision-relevant questions, and tailoring products and timing to the audiences who can use the evidence. chapter-14-disseminating-results-and-achieving-policy-impact.qmd
15. Choosing a Sample 291-319 Sampling decisions determine whether an evaluation can detect meaningful impacts for the population and subgroups that matter to the policy question. chapter-15-choosing-a-sample.qmd
16. Finding Adequate Sources of Data 321-347 Impact evaluations need data sources that measure outcomes, intermediate outcomes, implementation, and context with enough quality, timing, and coverage to support causal claims. chapter-16-finding-adequate-sources-of-data.qmd

Legacy Extracts

  • What Is Impact Evaluation?
  • Constructing a Theory of Change
  • Causal Inference and Counterfactuals

References

Gertler, Paul J., Sebastian Martinez, Patrick Premand, Laura B. Rawlings, and Christel M. J. Vermeersch. 2016. Impact Evaluation in Practice. World Bank.
